description: Add a cookie-aware cross-origin fetch proxy to the extension. Chrome MV3 does not send HttpOnly cookies on cross-origin fetch requests from extension-injected MAIN world code (both file-based IIFE injection and inline chrome.scripting.executeScript). This blocks any plugin whose web app API is on a different subdomain from the page (e.g., linear.app -> client-api.linear.app). The fix: add a bidirectional message bridge so tool handlers can route fetch requests through the extension background service worker, which reads cookies via chrome.cookies.getAll() and attaches them as headers.

userStories.title: Add fetch:proxy message types and background handler
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I want the extension background to handle fetch:proxy requests so that plugin tools can make cross-origin authenticated requests via the background service worker.
acceptanceCriteria: New InternalMessage types added: 'fetch:proxy' (content script -> background) and 'fetch:proxy:response' (background -> content script via chrome.tabs.sendMessage)
fetch:proxy message carries: requestId (string), url (string), method (string), headers (Record<string,string>), body (string|undefined), pluginName (string)
fetch:proxy:response message carries: requestId (string), status (number), statusText (string), headers (Record<string,string>), body (string)
Background handler reads cookies via chrome.cookies.getAll({ url }) and builds a Cookie header from them
Background handler makes the actual fetch with the Cookie header + all original headers from the request
Background handler validates that the request URL's registrable domain matches at least one of the plugin's urlPatterns (security check) — reject with an error if not
fetch:proxy is NOT in EXTENSION_ONLY_TYPES (it comes from content scripts, same as tool:progress and plugin:logs)
Background handler is registered in the backgroundHandlers map
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run knip passes
npm run test passes
userStories.notes:
Files to modify:

1. `platform/browser-extension/src/extension-messages.ts` — add two new interfaces to the InternalMessage union:
   - `FetchProxyRequestMessage` with type 'fetch:proxy', fields: requestId, url, method, headers, body, pluginName
   - `FetchProxyResponseMessage` with type 'fetch:proxy:response', fields: requestId, status, statusText, headers, body, error?

2. `platform/browser-extension/src/background-message-handlers.ts` — add `handleFetchProxy` handler:
   - Extract pluginName from message, look up PluginMeta via getAllPluginMeta()
   - Validate the request URL's origin is same-site with at least one of the plugin's urlPatterns. Use `new URL(url).hostname` and check that it shares the same registrable domain (eTLD+1) as at least one pattern. A simple approach: extract the hostname from the pattern, then compare the last two domain segments (e.g., 'linear.app' from '*://linear.app/*' and 'client-api.linear.app' share 'linear.app')
   - Read cookies via `chrome.cookies.getAll({ url: message.url })` and build a `Cookie` header string from `name=value` pairs joined by '; '
   - Make `fetch(url, { method, headers: { ...originalHeaders, Cookie: cookieHeader }, body })` from the background service worker
   - Read the response: status, statusText, response headers (via `Object.fromEntries(response.headers.entries())`), and body text via `response.text()`
   - Send the response back via `chrome.tabs.sendMessage(senderTabId, { type: 'fetch:proxy:response', requestId, status, statusText, headers, body })`. The sender tab ID comes from the `sender` parameter of the chrome.runtime.onMessage callback — this is available in the `handleMessage` function in `initBackgroundMessageHandlers`. Currently the handler functions only receive `(message, sendResponse)`. The handler signature needs to be extended to also pass the sender tab ID. The cleanest approach: change MessageHandler type to include sender info, or pass tabId as part of the message from the ISOLATED world relay (recommended — have the ISOLATED world script add `tabId: chrome.runtime.id` or use `sender.tab?.id` in the background). Actually, `sender.tab.id` is already available in the `chrome.runtime.onMessage` listener at line 596 — just pass it to the handler. Change the `MessageHandler` type to `(message, sendResponse, senderTabId?: number)` and pass `sender.tab?.id` from the listener.
   - Add 'fetch:proxy' to the backgroundHandlers map
   - Do NOT add 'fetch:proxy' to EXTENSION_ONLY_TYPES (it comes from content scripts)
   - Wrap fetch in try/catch — on network error, send back { type: 'fetch:proxy:response', requestId, status: 0, statusText: '', headers: {}, body: '', error: errorMessage }
   - Add a 30-second timeout via AbortSignal.timeout(30_000)

Security: The URL domain validation prevents a compromised page from using the proxy to make authenticated requests to arbitrary domains. A plugin can only proxy-fetch to domains that share the same registrable domain as its declared urlPatterns.

userStories.title: Add bidirectional ISOLATED world bridge for fetch proxy in tool dispatch
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I want the tool dispatch to inject an ISOLATED world bridge that relays fetch:proxy requests from MAIN world to the background and responses back, using the same CustomEvent pattern as progress reporting.
acceptanceCriteria: A new function injectFetchProxyBridge(tabId, dispatchId) injects an ISOLATED world script that listens for CustomEvent 'opentabs:fetch-proxy:request:{dispatchId}' on document

The ISOLATED world listener extracts requestId, url, method, headers, body, pluginName from the event detail and sends them to the background via chrome.runtime.sendMessage({ type: 'fetch:proxy', ... })

The ISOLATED world script also listens for chrome.runtime.onMessage with type 'fetch:proxy:response' and matching requestId, then dispatches a CustomEvent 'opentabs:fetch-proxy:response:{requestId}' on document with the response data
A removeFetchProxyBridge(tabId, dispatchId) function cleans up the ISOLATED world listeners (same pattern as removeProgressListener)
injectFetchProxyBridge is called before executeToolOnTab and removeFetchProxyBridge is called after (same lifecycle as progress listener)
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run knip passes
npm run test passes
userStories.notes:
Files to modify:

`platform/browser-extension/src/tool-dispatch.ts`

Follow the exact pattern of `injectProgressListener` / `removeProgressListener` (lines 71-130).

The ISOLATED world script needs two listeners:
1. `document.addEventListener('opentabs:fetch-proxy:request:' + dId, handler)` — relays request to background via `chrome.runtime.sendMessage({ type: 'fetch:proxy', ...detail })`
2. `chrome.runtime.onMessage.addListener(handler)` — listens for `{ type: 'fetch:proxy:response' }` messages from background and dispatches `new CustomEvent('opentabs:fetch-proxy:response:' + msg.requestId, { detail: msg })` on document

The cleanup function should remove both listeners.

Important: the chrome.runtime.onMessage listener in ISOLATED world must filter by `type === 'fetch:proxy:response'` and should only handle messages whose requestId matches a pending request (to avoid cross-talk between concurrent tool dispatches). A simple approach: maintain a Set of pending requestIds in the ISOLATED world closure.

The lifecycle in the executeToolOnTab call site (around line 386):
```
await injectProgressListener(tid, dispatchId);
await injectFetchProxyBridge(tid, dispatchId);
try { ... } finally { removeProgressListener(tid, dispatchId); removeFetchProxyBridge(tid, dispatchId); }
```

userStories.title: Add fetchViaBackground to ToolHandlerContext and wire it in the MAIN world dispatch func
userStories.description: As a plugin developer, I want a fetchViaBackground(url, init) method on ToolHandlerContext so that I can make cross-origin authenticated requests that include cookies without needing to know about the proxy mechanism.
acceptanceCriteria: ToolHandlerContext in platform/plugin-sdk/src/index.ts gains a new method: fetchViaBackground(url: string, init?: RequestInit): Promise<{ status: number; statusText: string; headers: Record<string, string>; body: string; ok: boolean }>

The MAIN world dispatch func in tool-dispatch.ts constructs context.fetchViaBackground that dispatches a CustomEvent 'opentabs:fetch-proxy:request:{dispatchId}' with a unique requestId and returns a Promise
The Promise resolves when a 'opentabs:fetch-proxy:response:{requestId}' CustomEvent is received on document
The requestId is generated uniquely per call (e.g., dispatchId + ':' + counter or crypto.randomUUID())
The init parameter supports method, headers, and body (serialized to string if object)
The response object includes ok (status >= 200 && status < 300), status, statusText, headers, and body as a string
If the response contains an error field (network failure), the Promise rejects with a descriptive error
A 30-second timeout is applied — if no response CustomEvent is received within 30s, the Promise rejects
npm run build passes
npm run type-check passes
npm run lint passes
npm run knip passes
npm run test passes
